serving.natural_language_portion: One medium raw zucchini (approximately 196 g) is a typical single serving consumed as a snack, crudité, or salad ingredient.

nutrient_source.title: Squash, summer, zucchini, includes skin, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169291)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for raw zucchini with skin matches the input food exactly. HPB Singapore values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, and iron are consistent with USDA data and are retained as primary values per input provenance. USDA SR Legacy used to supplement saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, and water content.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Raw zucchini is a non-starchy vegetable with very low carbohydrate content (3.11 g/100g). The University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care) classify non-starchy summer squash / courgette as low GI, with a GI of approximately 15. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (15 × 6.1 g per 196 g serving) / 100 ≈ 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (8 mg/100g) and high potassium (261 mg/100g) yielding a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio
hypertension.guidance: Raw zucchini is an excellent choice for hypertensive individuals; its sodium content of 8 mg/100g is negligible and its potassium content of 261 mg/100g actively supports vasodilation and blood pressure reduction consistent with DASH dietary guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (3.11 g/100g), low GI (~15), and high water content minimising postprandial glucose excursion
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw zucchini is highly suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; its glycemic index of approximately 15 and carbohydrate load of 3.11 g/100g produce a negligible glycemic load per serving (~0.9), making it a safe, high-volume, low-energy vegetable for blood glucose management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.08 g/100g), zero cholesterol, and potassium-rich profile supporting cardiac function
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw zucchini is cardioprotective by virtue of its negligible saturated fat (0.08 g/100g), zero cholesterol, low energy density (17 kcal/100g), and potassium content (261 mg/100g) that supports healthy cardiac rhythm and blood pressure, consistent with AHA dietary recommendations for cardiovascular risk reduction.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Non-nightshade vegetable; provides vitamin C (17.9 mg/100g) as an antioxidant; no pro-inflammatory components
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Zucchini is safe for rheumatoid arthritis patients as it is not a nightshade vegetable and contains no solanine; however, it lacks concentrated anti-inflammatory factors such as omega-3 fatty acids, polyphenols, or >3 g/100g dietary fibre, so it should be consumed as part of a broader anti-inflammatory dietary pattern rather than relied upon as a primary RA-management food.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Zucchini (also known as courgette) is a tender summer squash of the species Cucurbita pepo, consumed raw or cooked. In its raw form, it presents as a firm, cylindrical, green-skinned vegetable with pale, moist flesh and a mild, slightly sweet flavour with a crisp texture. Raw zucchini requires no preparation beyond washing and slicing, making it a convenient low-calorie snack or salad ingredient. Nutritionally, raw zucchini is exceptionally low in energy (17 kcal/100g) and carbohydrates (3.11 g/100g), with a glycemic index of approximately 15, placing it firmly in the low-GI category. It provides 1.21 g protein, 0.32 g fat, and 1.0 g dietary fibre per 100g, alongside meaningful amounts of potassium (261 mg), vitamin C (17.9 mg), folate (24 mcg DFE), and magnesium (18 mg). Its sodium content is negligible at 8 mg/100g. These properties make raw zucchini an ideal vegetable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher, and fits low-carbohydrate, ketogenic, and DASH dietary patterns. Zucchini is widely used in Mediterranean, Middle Eastern, and Western cuisines and is increasingly popular in Singapore and Southeast Asia as a health-conscious vegetable choice.
common_user_queries: How many calories in raw zucchini?
What is the glycemic index of zucchini?
Is courgette the same as zucchini?
Is zucchini good for blood pressure?
What nutrients are in raw zucchini?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore (confidence 0.95) and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169291) for raw zucchini with skin. Micronutrient values supplemented from USDA SR Legacy exact match. GI value sourced from University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008). All values are internally consistent and supported by approved primary sources.
notes: cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): zucchini is a plant food and inherently contains no cholesterol per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero): plant foods do not synthesise vitamin B12. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero): raw zucchini contains negligible vitamin D per USDA SR Legacy. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (naturally zero): unprocessed plant foods do not contain industrially produced trans fats; any naturally occurring trans fat in plant foods is below detection threshold. Saturated fat (0.08 g/100g), monounsaturated fat (0.01 g/100g), polyunsaturated fat (0.09 g/100g), vitamin C (17.9 mg/100g), vitamin A (10 mcg RAE/100g), B-vitamins, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water content supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 169291 where not present in HPB input. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database classification of non-starchy summer squash / courgette as low GI per Atkinson et al. (2008), Diabetes Care. Rheumatoid arthritis health context included as an additional condition from the input record. The input record's health_suitability for rheumatoid_arthritis listed suitable=false; this has been corrected to concern_level=low with appropriate clinical nuance — zucchini is safe for RA patients but is not a primary anti-inflammatory food. Ontology codes: FoodOn URI and SNOMED CT code are defensible matches; FoodEx2 code A00GJ is the EFSA FoodEx2 code for courgette/zucchini raw. Reviewers should verify FoodEx2 code A00GJ against the current EFSA FoodEx2 catalogue if this record is used in a European regulatory context.
